reasoning: The effective yield on B-rated US High Yield corporate debt spiked from around 7.2% in March to 8.71% in early April 2025 primarily due to new tariff announcements and "trade war fears." However, the market has rapidly digested this news, causing yields to drop sharply back towards their pre-spike baseline, reaching 7.63% on April 25, 2025 (dropping an average of ~5-10 bps per day recently). Given this strong downward momentum, the likelihood of a reversal pushing yields back above the April 27th baseline (expected to be ~7.63%) by the short-term horizons (May 4, May 27) is very low. For longer horizons (July and October), there is increased uncertainty due to the potential for further macroeconomic shocks, unpriced recession risks, or subsequent spikes in credit spreads as trade realities manifest, which pulls the probability upward toward 0.5 with time.
